faq.question: What is your outcome variable and how do you measure it?
faq.author: C. Gerber

faq.question: What is your unit of analysis?
faq.author: C. Gerber

faq.question: Is your outcome variable not a biased measure?
faq.author: C. Gerber

faq.question: Are your factor and outcome variable not correlated?
faq.author: C. Gerber

quotes.text: Answer your own research question. If multiple answers, pick the one you like most, link it to literature and repeat.
quotes.author: C. Gerber

quotes.text: If the results in WoS are more than 500, it's too much.
quotes.author: C. Gerber

quotes.text: The F statistic tells you if any of the variables play a significant role. The H0 is that all coefficients are 0.
A control factor is used when other factors then the ones I consider are also affecting the outcome variable.
There are 2 limitations to the variables you consider: they must be theory driven and sufficient data must exist.
When your outcome variable is not continuous, you can consider discrete choice models.
The best way to put forward a theory is in a mathematical equation.
quotes.author: C. Gerber

quotes.text: Take a global north idea and consider why it doesn't apply to the global south or SSA.
Every decision you make must be argued and requires a reference.
quotes.author: C. Gerber

quotes.text: If two or more of your predictors (X variables) are highly correlated with each other, it becomes hard for the model to separate their individual effects on Y.
Reflecting on your outcome variable and how you measure it gives you perspective and makes your argument flow.
quotes.author: C. Gerber

quotes.text: Your outcome variable should not change frequently. Your input variables might change more often.
quotes.author: C. Gerber

quotes.text: Like artists are trained by first copying from masters, copy constructs, how constructs relate, arguments and then bring them towards something original.
quotes.author: C. Gerber

quotes.text: Understand the theory the academic construct is a part of.
quotes.author: C. Gerber
